Abstract
--------

CRISPR-Cas (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats-CRISPR-associated proteins) has undergone marked advancements since its discovery as an adaptive immune system in bacteria and archaea, emerged as a potent gene-editing tool after the successful engineering of its synthetic guide RNA (sgRNA) toward the targeting of specific DNA sequences with high accuracy. Besides its DNA editing ability, further-developed Cas variants can also edit the epigenome, rendering the CRISPR-Cas system a versatile tool for genome and epigenome manipulation and a pioneering force in precision medicine. This review explores the latest advancements in CRISPR-Cas technology and its therapeutic and biomedical applications, highlighting its transformative impact on precision medicine. Moreover, the current status of CRISPR therapeutics in clinical trials is discussed. Finally, we address the persisting challenges and prospects of CRISPR-Cas technology.

**Keywords:** CRISPR-Cas9, genome editing, epigenome modulation, cancer immunotherapy, and CRISPR in clinical trials

1 Introduction
--------------

CRISPR-Cas systems are an acquired immune system of most bacteria and archaea, protecting them from invading viruses, bacteriophages or mobile genetic elements ([Jinek et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B105)). The critical components of the CRISPR-Cas system include CRISPR-associated (Cas) proteins and the CRISPR array ([Huang et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B94)). Cas9, a well-characterized protein in this system, features endonuclease domains (HNH and RuvC) and a gRNA-binding domain (REC) ([Wang et al., 2022a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B257)). The CRISPR array consists of short repetitive DNA sequences separated by spacers derived from prior infections ([Doudna and Charpentier, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B44)).

Upon re-infection, the array is transcribed into precursor CRISPR RNA (pre-crRNA) and processed into mature crRNAs, which, along with trans-activating CRISPR RNA (tracrRNA), guide Cas proteins to the target DNA. A protospacer adjacent motif (PAM) site adjacent to the target is recognised for binding, enabling Cas proteins to introduce double-stranded breaks (DSBs) at the target site and inactivating the virus ([Jore et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B108); [Barrangou and Marraffini, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B13)).

The mechanistic discovery of CRISPR-Cas’s principle of action over decades of research culminated in 2012 in the engineering of crRNAs into a single, synthetic guide RNA designed to target specific sequences, with the potential of developing as a versatile genome editing tool ([Gasiunas et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B68); [Jinek et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B105)). Shortly after, it was demonstrated that CRISPR-Cas9 technology enables effective and targeted genome editing in mammalian cells when they showed that DSBs created by Cas9 trigger cellular DNA repair pathways such as non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) or homology-directed repair (HDR). NHEJ often results in insertions or deletions (indels), causing gene disruptions ([Silva et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B230)). HDR, on the other hand, is a high-fidelity DNA repair pathway that can be used with a donor template to induce particular genetic alterations ([Liao et al., 2024](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B147)).

This groundbreaking innovation has enabled accurate and specified genomic modifications more easily adapted than traditional gene editing techniques, such as zinc-finger nucleases (ZFNs) and transcription activator-like effector nucleases (TALENs) ([Gaj et al., 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B62)). While ZFNs and TALENs can also facilitate targeted genomic editing, but they are limited by their complex and labour-intensive design and assembly processes, which are less practical for rapid and versatile applications. In contrast, CRISPR technology offers a simpler, more efficient, and highly adaptable system. Its guide RNA-based targeting mechanism allows quick design and broad applicability across different genomic targets ([Gupta and Musunuru, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B77)). This feature has given CRISPR-Cas technology tremendous adaptability and utility in numerous biological areas such as genetic engineering, functional genomics, and medicinal development.

The present study comprehensively reviews the latest advances in CRISPR-Cas technology and its applications, underscoring its revolutionary impact on precision medicine. Its medical applications have been divided into therapeutic and biomedical. The therapeutic use of CRISPR-Cas-based genome and epigenome editing includes correcting genetic disorders, antiviral therapy, and eliminating antimicrobial resistance. It has been widely applied in oncology due to its efficiency in engineering chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T cell) therapies and oncolytic viruses, targeting oncogenes, and modifying the tumour microenvironment. Biomedical applications include drug target discovery, modelling diseases, regenerative medicine, tissue engineering, cell reprogramming, and medical diagnostics. Finally, the current status of CRISPR therapeutics in clinical trials and the remaining challenges in the applications of CRISPR-Cas are discussed.

2 An overview of CRISPR-Cas system
----------------------------------

### 2.1 CRISPR-Cas systems, classes, and types

CRISPR-Cas systems exhibit significant diversity across prokaryotic organisms, and they are categorised into two major classes and six types based on their components and mechanisms of action ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#F1)). Class 1 systems, encompassing Types I, III, and IV, feature multi-protein effector complexes, while Class 2 systems, comprising Types II, V, and VI, are defined by single-protein effectors ([Mohanraju et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B173)).

#### FIGURE 1.

[![Image 10: FIGURE 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/7961/11669675/25c7437ed0a8/fgeed-06-1509924-g001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=11669675_fgeed-06-1509924-g001.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/figure/F1/)

Schematic representation of CRISPR-Cas systems, categorised into Class 1 and Class 2. Each class is further divided into types and subtypes, showing the associated complexes and their primary targets.

Class 1 systems are more complex, with multiple proteins contributing to their function. Among these, Type I systems, such as Type I-A, utilise a CRISPR-associated complex for antiviral defence, Cascade (CRISPR-associated complex for antiviral defence), to recognise and bind complementary sequences in dsDNA. The Cas3 helicase-nuclease is recruited upon target recognition to unwind and directionally degrade the DNA. This system has been studied for its role in bacterial immunity but is less developed for genome editing due to its complexity ([Yoshimi and Mashimo, 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B296)).

Type III systems, such as Type III-A, use the Csm/Cmr complex to target single-stranded RNA (ssRNA) while also engaging in a secondary DNase activity that targets nearby dsDNA, adding a layer of defence. These systems can cleave RNA and DNA, with applications in bacterial immunity and potentially in antiviral therapeutic approaches. However, the dual cleavage activity complicates its use in precision genome editing ([Paraan et al., 2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B191)). Type IV systems, like Type IV-A, also target dsDNA, but their detailed mechanisms remain less elucidated ([Xu and Li, 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B282)).

Class 2 systems, which are simpler due to their single-protein effectors, have been the focus of most genome editing research. Class 2 systems include the well-studied Type II, with SpCas9 from _Streptococcus_ pyogenes being a prominent member. SpCas9 has become the archetype for genome editing, with its precise DNA recognition and cleavage guided by sgRNA, targeting sequences adjacent to PAM, typically NGG, which is necessary for Cas9 binding and activation ([Marraffini, 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B166)).

Type V systems, such as Type V-A (Cas12a/Cpf1), employ a single RuvC endonuclease domain to cleave target dsDNA in a PAM-dependent manner, followed by non-target strand cleavage ([Liao et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B146); [Paul and Montoya, 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B194)). Cas12a requires a T-rich PAM (e.g., TTTV), broadening the range of editable sequences compared to SpCas9’s G-rich PAM. Cas12a also performs staggered cuts in DNA, generating “sticky ends” that are advantageous for certain genetic modifications. Moreover, Cas12a can process its crRNA array independently, enabling more efficient multiplexed genome editing without requiring additional tracrRNA sequences ([Paul and Montoya, 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B194)).

Lastly, type VI systems, represented by Cas13a, are distinct in their ability to target and cleave ssRNA rather than DNA ([Watanabe et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B267)). Upon binding to its target RNA, Cas13a exhibits a unique collateral cleavage activity, indiscriminately cutting nearby non-target RNAs ([Liu L. et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B154)). The structural and functional classifications of CRISPR-Cas systems are elaborated in greater detail in ([Makarova and Koonin, 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B164)). The CRISPR-Cas systems continue to be refined for both basic research and clinical use, with each type offering unique properties that can be tailored to specific applications.

### 2.2 Technological advances in CRISPR-Cas nucleases and editing strategies

#### 2.2.1 Genome editing tools

Over the past 30 years, CRISPR-Cas technology has evolved dramatically, significantly advancing the field of genetic engineering and emphasising its versatility and precision ([Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#F2)). The foundational Cas9 nuclease marked a turning point by enabling targeted DSBs in DNA, which facilitated precise gene editing ([Jinek et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B105)). Due to the simplicity of its action and design, Cas9 has been recognised as a powerful tool in research and clinical applications.

##### FIGURE 2.

[![Image 11: FIGURE 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/7961/11669675/0c8e96918ec6/fgeed-06-1509924-g002.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=11669675_fgeed-06-1509924-g002.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/figure/F2/)

This figure displays a range of Cas protein variants and engineered tools derived from the CRISPR-Cas family, highlighting their diverse applications in genome and transcriptome editing. Central to the diagram is the native Cas9-sgRNA complex, which serves as a foundation for multiple modifications. Shown clockwise from the top left, base and prime editors (e.g., cytosine and adenine base editors, prime editors) allow single-nucleotide changes and precise insertions without DSBs. Recombinant Cas9 proteins (Cas9-Rad51/Rad52 fusions) enhance homology-directed repair (HDR) outcomes, while high-fidelity Cas9 variants reduce off-target effects to improve targeting specificity. Transcriptional activators (CRISPRa) and repressors (CRISPRi) enable gene expression modulation without altering DNA sequences, using dCas9 fused to transcriptional regulators. The dCAS9-APEX2 complex, tags nearby proteins with biotin, which is useful in proteome mapping. PAM-expanded Cas9 variants (e.g., SpRY, SpCas9-NG) target a broader range of PAM sequences, increasing flexibility in target selection. In the lower-left corner, “Natural Cas9 Variants” encompass Cas12, which produces sticky-end DNA cuts; Cas13, an RNA editor targeting RNA without modifying DNA; and Cas14, a small-size protein that cuts single-stranded DNA, independent of PAM recognition. This diverse toolkit illustrates the flexibility and breadth of CRISPR-Cas systems for targeted genetic and epigenetic modifications across various applications.

The traditional CRISPR-Cas9 editing relies on inducing DSBs at target sites. These DSBs can be repaired through the error-prone NHEJ pathway, probably leading to indels, or through HDR, which can be exploited to precisely incorporate donor DNA templates, rendering it a potent gene-editing tool in the correction of genetic disorders ([Xue and Greene, 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B283)). However, the native Cas9 has several limitations despite its utility, such as off-target effects and strict dependence on NGG PAM sequences, which can restrict editing in certain genomic regions ([Zhang et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B307); [Sato and Kuroda, 2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B219)).

The research innovations have focused on improving the specificity and efficiency of CRISPR-Cas systems. Some modified versions of Cas9, like high-fidelity Cas9 (SpCas9-HF1) and enhanced specificity Cas9 (eSpCas9), have been developed with decreased off-target effects by modulating protein-DNA interaction dynamics which minimise unintended edits in non-target genomic regions ([Huang et al., 2022b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B96)). Studies have discovered that these high-fidelity Cas9 mutants achieve improved specificity due to a proofreading mechanism that keeps them inactive when encountering mismatched DNA sequences. This mechanism prevents unintended cuts, enhancing the precision of genome editing ([Mengstie et al., 2024](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B169)).

Another effective approach to minimise off-target effects is using CRISPR nickases, which modify one nuclease domain to cut only a single DNA strand. Unlike standard Cas9, which creates DSBs, Cas9 nickase introduces single-stranded “nicks” that cells can promptly repair, reducing collateral damage and off-target mutations ([Trevino and Zhang, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B246)). Paired nickases—targeting both DNA strands but separately, enhance precision by creating DSB-like edits with minimised off-target effects, making them highly advantageous for accurate genome editing ([Torella et al., 2024](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B243), p. 1).

Furthermore, expanding the target sequence recognition depending on different PAM sequences has broadened its applications. The traditional Cas9 nucleases entirely depend on the PAM sequence—specifically, the NGG. This dependence on a specific PAM motif can be a significant limitation, as it restricts the range of possible target sites across the genome, particularly in genomic regions where the NGG motif is sparse or absent. This limitation has spurred the development of PAM-less or PAM-relaxed Cas9 variants, with SpRY as one of the most advanced examples. Engineered through mutations in its PAM-interacting domain, SpRY can recognise a broader range of PAM sequences, including NRN and NYN (where “R” is a purine and “Y” is a pyrimidine) ([Hibshman et al., 2024](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B87)). This flexibility allows SpRY to target nearly any genomic site, greatly expanding editing possibilities, particularly in AT-rich regions where NGG motifs are sparse ([Walton et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B253)). In addition to SpRY, other Cas9 variants with expanded PAM compatibility have been developed, including SpCas9-NG, which targets NG PAMs, and xCas9, which also recognises NG, GAA, and GAT PAMs ([Hu et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B92); [Hua et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B93)). While these variants improve targeting flexibility, SpRY represents a more substantial breakthrough due to its ability to eliminate the PAM constraint effectively. This advancement has significant implications for therapeutic genome editing, as it provides greater flexibility to target disease-causing mutations regardless of PAM availability ([Liang et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B144)). Variants like SpCas9-HF1 and eSpCas9 minimise off-target edits, and SpRY and SpCas9-NG enable expanded PAM Compatibility. However, while improving specificity, some high-fidelity Cas9 variants might suffer from reduced cutting efficiency ([Moreb et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B174); [Kulcsár et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B130)). Further optimisation is required to balance specificity and activity. Strategies such as fine-tuning protein-DNA interaction dynamics or enhancing guide RNA design could help improve cleavage efficiency while maintaining high specificity ([Ryan et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B216)).

Base editors enable targeted single-base pair conversions, thereby reducing the off-target effects and improving the accuracy of genome editing, hence very useful for correcting genetic disorders caused by point mutations ([Komor et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B127); [Liang et al., 2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B145)). Base editors enable the accurate correction of point mutations and eliminate the need for donor templates. However, potential unintended base conversions can still occur at nearby bases and can only change one base at a time, limiting their application to large-scale edits ([Doman et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B42); [Jeong et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B102)).

Prime editors enable several genome editing options, including base substitutions, insertions, and deletions of longer DNA sequences. Prime editors utilise a Cas9 nickase (nCas9) fused with a reverse transcriptase and a prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA), offering greater versatility for a broader range of genetic modifications ([Anzalone et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B8)). These editors represent a significant advancement in Cas9 engineering and a wide array of gene editing applications, including base substitutions, insertions, and deletions with precision. Also, the risk of off-target mutagenesis and safety for therapeutic applications is much reduced compared to other editing systems ([Anzalone et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B8); [Chen and Liu, 2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B29)). However, reduced efficiency, the need for optimisation for different cell types and the complex design of pegRNA and its components make it more technically demanding ([Zhao et al., 2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B309)).

Further advancements include developing alternative CRISPR systems, such as Cas12 and Cas13. Cas12, which makes staggered cuts in DNA, is particularly useful for insertions and genetic modifications that require sticky ends. Its smaller size and distinct PAM requirements make it a versatile tool for applications where Cas9’s blunt cuts and NGG PAM are limiting ([Zetsche et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B299); [Chen et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B28)).

Cas14 is an ultra-small CRISPR-associated protein discovered in certain archaea, notable for its size, typically under 70 kDa, compared to the larger Cas9 and Cas12 proteins ([Savage, 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B221)). This smaller size makes Cas14 advantageous for gene-editing applications requiring compact delivery systems, like adeno-associated viruses (AAVs), which have limited payload capacity ([Harrington et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B83)). Cas14 uniquely targets single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) via target binding with the gRNA without the need for a protospacer adjacent motif (PAM), broadening its potential target range and making it highly versatile for applications in diagnostics, gene therapy, and microbial studies ([Harrington et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B83)).

On the other hand, identifying RNA targeting Cas13 opened a new avenue for RNA editing. Indeed, the RNA editing potential of Cas13 has been exploited to detect viral RNA, including SARS-CoV-2, using its collateral cleavage activity targeting viral RNA sequences ([Abudayyeh et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B1); [Zhou et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B313)). This mechanism has been leveraged for molecular diagnostics, such as the detection of viral RNA, and holds promise in developing treatments for RNA viruses ([Yin et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B294)). Cas13a′s specificity for RNA without DNA modification makes it an attractive tool for transient knockdowns in research and potential therapeutic applications for diseases involving RNA, such as certain viral infections and neurodegenerative disorders ([Makarova and Koonin, 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B164)). While Cas13’s RNA specificity is ideal for transient knockdowns and diagnostics, its collateral cleavage can sometimes result in off-target RNA degradation, limiting its therapeutic potential ([Ai et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11669675/#B4); [Li et al., 2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1166967

# Future of CRISPR: Gene Editing Technologies Herald Landmark Clinical Trials

![Genetic Signatures for Cancer are Making Headlines](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2018/12/dna_291506.jpg)

Clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR), building on earlier-developed techniques such as zinc finger nucleases (ZFNs) and transcription activator-like effector nucleases (TALENs), has emerged as the most frequently used method of genome editing, with many new studies and trials gaining traction.1  
  
CRISPR technology includes a variety of methods, each suitable for different applications.2 CRISPR-Cas9, derived from the immune system of *Streptococcus pyogenes* bacteria, is the most widely used CRISPR-associated protein to date. Functioning as molecular scissors, CRISPR-Cas9 cuts DNA at specific sites, led by a programmable guide RNA that targets specific genetic sequences for disruption or correction.

CRISPR-Cas12, previously known as Cpf1, is another commonly-used variant. It provides certain advantages over Cas9, such as requiring a shorter guide RNA and producing a staggered cut, making it useful in diagnostic applications. CRISPR-Cas3, useful for extensive removal of DNA, is used to eliminate viral genomes from infected cells.

A relatively new addition to the research front, CRISPR-Cas13 targets RNA rather than DNA and is being explored for applications such as RNA degradation in influenza and SARS-CoV-2. Base editors and prime editors — derivatives of CRISPR technology — enable direct, single-nucleotide edits without introducing double-stranded breaks, which minimizes unintended effects.  
  
Late 2023 saw a significant milestone in genome editing with the Food and Drug Administration (FDA)’s first approval of a CRISPR application for clinical use.3 The CRISPR-Cas9 drug, Casgevy® (exagamglogene autotemcel or exa-cel), is indicated for treatment of both sickle cell disease (SCD) and transfusion-dependent beta thalassemia (TDT).

## Blood Diseases

Developed by Vertex Pharmaceuticals and CRISPR Therapeutics, exa-cel was approved on the basis of phase 3 trial data (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT03745287](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT03745287)) and functions by editing a gene to reactivate fetal hemoglobin production.4 This action alleviates the defective red blood cells responsible for SCD. Sixteen of 17 patients in the study stopped experiencing vaso-occlusive crises. Among the 27 patients with TDT, 25 were no longer dependent on transfusions. In some cases, these results were observed for more than 3 years.5

Although the mutations causing SCD and TDT are not directly corrected, the treatment effectively manages the disease long term. Chemotherapy is used before treatment to prepare for the integration of genetically modified cells, as exa-cel involves ex vivo editing techniques. CRISPR Therapeutics is exploring the potential for in vivo editing, which could reduce or eliminate the need for intensive chemotherapy and lower overall chemotherapy doses.  
  
Other researchers are also examining CRISPR for SCD. Editas Medicine is conducting phase 1/2 trials (RUBY; ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04853576](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04853576)) using Cas12a to activate fetal hemoglobin production, showing notable clinical benefits without serious adverse events.6 Beam Therapeutics has initiated a phase 1/2 trial (BEACON; ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05456880](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05456880)) using a base editing technique to enhance fetal hemoglobin without causing double-stranded DNA breaks, potentially minimizing safety risks.5 A University of California Consortium-led non-profit initiative is addressing direct repair of the SCD mutation, though it faces manufacturing delays.7

## Urinary Tract Infections

Beyond blood diseases, CRISPR applications extend to treating common yet challenging conditions such as urinary tract infections (UTIs), which can recur frequently and become antibiotic resistant. Locus Biosciences is recruiting approximately 800 participants for a phase 2/3 trial (ELIMINATE; ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05488340](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05488340)) to address UTIs caused by antimicrobial resistant *Escherichia coli*.8

LBP-EC01, a cocktail of 3 bacteriophages with CRISPR-Cas3, targets and destroys the genomes of the most common *E coli* strains, responsible for approximately 95% of UTIs. This therapy, which degrades DNA rather than repairing it, was first tested in a US-based phase 1b trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04191148](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04191148)) completed in February 2021 — the first to apply CRISPR technology in treating bacterial infections.9

## Hereditary Transthyretin Amyloidosis

CRISPR technology is also making strides in tackling complex genetic diseases. The first CRISPR-Cas9 therapy administered in a lipid nanoparticle (LNP) for systemic delivery was NTLA-2001, developed by Intellia Therapeutics to treat hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis (hATTR), a fatal, genetic protein-folding disease caused by mutations in the TTR gene that leads to production of a malfunctioning protein.10 Lipid nanoparticles tend to accumulate in the liver, where most TTR protein synthesis also occurs, placing drug and target in close proximity. The drug is administered via a single IV dose.  
  
Clinical trials for NTLA-2001 began in late 2020, focusing on 2 patient groups: those with symptoms of neuropathy10 and those with cardiomyopathy (ClinicialTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04601051](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04601051)).11 The studies showed a significant reduction in toxic protein levels — over 85% even at the lowest dose, and more than 90% among patients receiving the highest dose. This reduction directly impacts the progression of hATTR, as lower TTR levels correlate with reduced amyloid deposits and, consequently, slower disease progression.  
  
Researchers are adding a mid-range dose for further investigation. Intellia is recruiting for a phase 3 double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT06128629](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT06128629)) to assess NTLA-2001 for cardiomyopathy.12

## Hereditary Angioedema

Intellia is also advancing a second CRISPR-Cas9 drug delivered through lipid nanoparticles, NTLA-2002, to address hereditary angioedema (HAE).13 HAE is marked by recurrent, severe swelling attacks that can be life-threatening, especially when the airways are involved. The therapy targets the *KLKB1* gene, which plays a role in the production of bradykinin, a peptide that promotes the characteristic inflammation and vascular permeability of HAE.

The treatment is delivered via a 1-time intravenous administration,13 and clinical trials (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05120830](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05120830)) are evaluating 3 different dosage levels among 10 participants. Early results have shown a significant decrease in the frequency of attacks, with the highest dosage achieving more than a 90% reduction in the inflammatory protein levels.

Intellia is moving the therapy into a global phase 3 trial in 2024 to confirm the results on a broader scale and assess the treatment’s impact across diverse populations.14

## Cardiovascular Disease

The results seen in hereditary angioedema may lead to a broader application of CRISPR in other serious conditions. Trials for cardiovascular diseases, type 1 diabetes, cancers, autoimmune diseases, and HIV are already underway, in earlier phases.

In one instance, a gene editing treatment for familial hypercholesterolemia, developed by Verve Therapeutics, is progressing through a phase 1 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05398029](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05398029?a=1)) to address high cholesterol levels.15 The drug, Verve-101, uses base editing delivered through lipid nanoparticles to modify the *PCSK9* gene in the liver, aiming to reduce cholesterol without the double-stranded breaks typically associated with CRISPR-Cas9.

Early results have shown a significant reduction in low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, exceeding traditional statin effects. Investigators have reported significant cardiovascular events among participants, prompting further data requirements. A phase 2 trial for either Verve-101 or Verve-102, which uses an identical gene editing mechanism but different lipid nanoparticle delivery system, is planned for 2025.16

Simultaneously, CRISPR Therapeutics has begun phase 1 trials targeting angiopoietin-like 3 protein and lipoprotein(a), which are linked to cardiovascular risks.17 These trials also use lipid nanoparticles and focus on more prevalent genetic variants, potentially broadening applications in heart health.

## Type 1 Diabetes

CRISPR also presents possibilities for the management of chronic metabolic diseases such as type 1 diabetes. In partnership with ViaCyte, who is no longer involved in the study,18 CRISPR Therapeutics initiated a phase 1 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05565248](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05565248)) targeting type 1 diabetes.19 The trial employs CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing to modify pancreatic cells derived from stem cells.

The key modification involves editing immune-related genes and placing them in a specialized pouch within the patient, making them less visible to the immune system. The treatment is designed to support nutrient and insulin exchange while reducing immune system attack, potentially eliminating the need for lifelong immunosuppression.

This approach may diffuse the many challenges associated with traditional pancreatic cell transplants, such as the risks for long-term immunosuppression and incompatibility of donor cells.19

## Systemic Lupus Erythematosus

Addressing another chronic disease, CRISPR Therapeutics is also conducting a pre-clinical trial study using next-generation CD19-targeting chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).20 This marks the first use of CRISPR gene-editing technology for autoimmune diseases, specifically adapting CAR T cells to target and temporarily deplete B cells.

Investigators face a major challenge as they attempt to alleviate SLE through B cell depletion without causing prolonged immunodeficiency or exacerbating the autoimmune condition. Unlike cancer treatments in which long-term CAR T cell persistence is critical, this approach for SLE transiently resets the immune system, potentially inducing remission without the need for continuous presence of these modified cells.

## HIV

Although HIV has become a chronic disease for many, CRISPR applications targeting HIV will pave the way for genome editing treatments for viral diseases. One new approach directly targets HIV DNA embedded in a host’s genome.21 Excision Biotherapeutics is conducting a phase 1 clinical trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05144386](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05144386)) for an HIV treatment that uses CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing technology.

In this process, guide RNAs direct the Cas9 enzyme to precisely excise key segments of the viral genome at 2 specific sites, aimed at completely removing the virus from infected cells. The CRISPR components are delivered via an adeno-associated virus 9 (AAV9) vector in a single infusion.

Initiated in July 2022, the trial has thus far involved dosing a small number of participants, with plans to increase the dosage contingent on initial safety results.21 This trial is among the first to test the potential of CRISPR technology for treating chronic viral infections.

## Blood Cancers

In the acute and critical care arena, advancements in gene editing for cancer have been among the most important gene editing applications.22 Following positive early-phase outcomes and improved manufacturing techniques that enhance CAR T cell yields, CRISPR Therapeutics has progressed to phase 1/2 trials (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifiers: [NCT05643742](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05643742), [NCT05795595](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05795595)) with their advanced CAR T cell therapies targeting CD70 and CD19 proteins, which are prevalent on various tumor and blood cancer cells.23 These updated therapies now feature additional edits to Regnase-1 and *TGFBR-2* genes to enhance T cell anti-cancer activity by deactivating genes that inhibit T cell functions.  
  
Caribou Biosciences has also seen success with its CAR T cells that target CD19, incorporating a PD-1 knockout to circumvent cancer cell immune evasion.24 Promising results from its phase 1 trial (ANTLER; ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04637763](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04637763)) showed sustained remission among a significant number of participants, prompting Caribou to prepare for a direct transition from phase 1 to phase 3 trials, aimed at combating aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphomas. CAR T therapies typically use CRISPR-Cas9.

## Challenges

Financially, Casgevy’s $2 million per-patient price tag may be met with resistance by insurance companies, and future drugs will likely be just as expensive.25.26 Formulary concerns will prompt robust cost-benefit analyses of these expenses against potential long-term savings from reduced hospital visits and chronic care.  
  
At the same time, regulatory bodies are struggling to keep pace with rapid advancements in gene therapy.25 Agencies will have to establish standards to manage the risks for potential off-target effects and other unintended genetic modifications. The ethical implications of gene editing, especially concerning edits that could be passed to future generations, require careful consideration and oversight.

As research continues, the focus will also be on rising to the challenges of affordability and regulatory compliance so that gene editing can be both effective and accessible across scientific and medical communities.  
  
Aljabali and colleagues25 note in their work on CRISPR-Cas9 technology, “As we traverse the vistas of continued advancements, converge in interdisciplinary collaborations, and orchestrate the confluence of nanomaterial integration, a vibrant symphony reverberates, a cadence that heralds transformative discoveries, novel therapies, and a reverberating symphony of improved human health outcomes.”

Abstract
--------

The clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR)-Cas (CRISPR-associated proteins) is an adaptive immune system present in most bacteria and archaea, protecting them from infection by exogenous genetic elements. Due to its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and precise gene editing capabilities, CRISPR/Cas technology has emerged as a promising tool for treating diseases. The continuous refinement of derivative systems has further broadened its scope in disease treatment. Nevertheless, the heterogeneous physiopathological nature of diseases and variations in disease onset sites pose significant challenges for _in vivo_ applications of CRISPR systems. The efficiency of CRISPR systems in disease treatment is directly influenced by the performance of the delivery system. Additionally, concerns such as off-target effects present crucial hurdles in the clinical implementation of CRISPR systems. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the development of CRISPR systems, vector technologies, and their applications in disease treatment, while also addressing the challenges encountered in clinical settings. Furthermore, future research directions are outlined to pave the way for advancements in CRISPR-based therapies.

1. Introduction
---------------

The CRISPR/Cas system, an abbreviation for Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats and CRISPR-associated proteins, is a naturally occurring adaptive immune mechanism found in bacteria and archaea. Its origin is rooted in the evolutionary arms race between these microorganisms and their viral predators, particularly bacteriophages[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib1). The CRISPR/Cas system operates by incorporating short sequences of viral DNA, derived from previous infections, into the CRISPR loci within the host genome. These sequences, known as spacers, are flanked by repeat sequences, forming a unique genetic architecture. When the host organism is re-exposed to the same virus, these spacers are transcribed into RNA molecules, which guide the associated Cas proteins to the corresponding viral DNA. The Cas proteins then cleave the viral DNA, thereby neutralizing the threat[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib1), [2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib2), [3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib3).

The classification of the CRISPR/Cas system is based on its structural and functional diversity. It is broadly categorized into two main classes, each with several types and subtypes (class 1: type I, III, IV; class 2: Type II, V, VI)[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib4), [5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib5), [6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib6). Therein, type II CRISPR/Cas9 system is a revolutionary gene-editing tool derived from the _Streptococcus pyogenes_[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib7). It comprises two main components: the CRISPR RNA (crRNA) and the Cas9 protein[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib8). The crRNA guides the Cas9 protein to the target DNA sequence through base-pairing, while the Cas9 protein cleaves the DNA at the specified location, leading to precise modifications. This system offers several advantages over previous gene-editing technologies, including its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and versatility[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib9), [10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib10). Unlike earlier methods such as zinc finger nucleases (ZFNs) and transcription activator-like effector nucleases (TALENs), CRISPR/Cas9 does not require custom protein engineering for each target sequence, making it more accessible to researchers[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib11), [12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib12), [13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib13). The applications of CRISPR/Cas9 span across various fields, including basic research, agriculture, and medicine[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib5), [14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib14), [15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib15). In disease treatment, CRISPR/Cas9 holds immense potential for correcting genetic mutations underlying various disorders, ranging from monogenic diseases to complex multifactorial conditions[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib16). Additionally, it enables the study of disease mechanisms through precise genome editing in cellular and animal models[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib16), [17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib17).

The development of CRISPR/Cas9 has catalyzed innovation in genome engineering, leading to the development of derivative systems with enhanced functionalities and tailored applications[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib4), [18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib18). These derivative systems typically leverage the foundational principles of CRISPR/Cas9 while incorporating modifications or additional components to address specific limitations or achieve novel functionalities in physiological regulation for specific diseases[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib19), [20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib20). Incorporation of derivative systems in the genome engineering toolbox offers unprecedented precision, versatility, and control over genome editing, with profound implications for therapeutic interventions.

While various versions of CRISPR systems enable molecular-level control, their ability to exert therapeutic effects at specific disease loci necessitates efficient delivery systems[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib21), [22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib22). Different diseases manifest in distinct tissue types, each with unique physiological barriers that impede the efficient delivery of therapeutic agents. For example, solid tumors exhibit abnormal vasculature, dense extracellular matrix, and elevated interstitial fluid pressure, limiting the penetration and distribution of therapeutic payloads[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib23), [24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib24). Similarly, neurological disorders involve the blood-brain barrier (BBB), which restricts the entry of macromolecules into the brain parenchyma[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib25), [26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib26). Furthermore, the biodistribution and pharmacokinetics of delivery vehicles influence their accumulation at disease sites, necessitating strategies to enhance specificity and retention while minimizing off-target effects[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib27), [28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib28), [29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib29). Addressing the complex interplay of physiological barriers and developing innovative delivery strategies are essential steps toward realizing their therapeutic potential inspecific disease contexts.

In this review, we have discussed the evolution of CRISPR technology, delineated advancements in delivery systems, and summarized the recent studies on the application of CRISPR systems in the treatment of multiple diseases. Furthermore, we have deliberated on the research frontiers and clinical challenges of CRISPR-based therapies in disease treatment. Prospectively, researchers can enhance the specificity, efficacy, and safety of CRISPR-based therapeutics by harnessing interdisciplinary approaches and leveraging emerging technologies, ultimately paving the way for precision medicine paradigms tailored to individual disease states.

2. The CRISPR/Cas9 system
-------------------------

CRISPR/Cas (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeat-associated protein) systems, an adaptive immune system, were initially discovered in prokaryotes[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib30). The CRISPR/Cas system comprises the CRISPR-encoding sequence, Cas-encoding sequence, and the CRISPR-associated sequence, which includes the leader, repeats, and spacers sequence[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib1), [3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib3), [31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib31). Over the last decade, it has transformed into a third-generation gene editing tool, profoundly influencing biomedical research[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib2), [32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib32), [33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib33). A DNA structure with numerous repetitions that reads the same forwards and backward was discovered in 1987 and dubbed CRISPR in 2002[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib34), [35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib35). In 2005, three investigations confirmed the CRISPR system as an adaptive immune system found in archaea[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib36), [37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib37), [38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib38). In 2008, research demonstrated that the CRISPR/Cas system was directed by a small segment of CRISPR RNAs to trigger an antiviral response in prokaryotes[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib39). Further research has shown the specificity of CRISPR for DNA targeting[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib40), [41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib41). In 2011, a trans-acting CRISPR RNA (tracrRNA) that is complementary to the pre-CRISPR RNA (pre-crRNA) was discovered[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib42). In 2012, researchers successfully cleaved target genes using recombinant Cas9 proteins, tracrRNAs, and crRNAs _in vitro_, confirming the system’s versatility in cleaving diverse double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) sites _via_ distinct guide RNAs[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib8). A significant milestone was reached in 2013 when scientists successfully performed gene editing in eukaryotes using the CRISPR/Cas9 system[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib43), [44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib44). Since its inception, ongoing research and development have propelled the CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing system’s impact on the life sciences field. The first clinical trial utilizing CRISPR/Cas9 was disclosed in China in 2016[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib45), [46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib46), and the technique was honored with the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2020 ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#fig1)).

The CRISPR/Cas system serves as the adaptive immune system in prokaryotes by cleaving target DNA, resulting in double-stranded DNA breaks (DSB) for effective gene editing. Exogenous DNA entering a bacterium triggers Cas-associated proteins to identify the protospacer adjacent motif (PAM, usually composed of NGG bases) and cleave the sequence near the PAM ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#fig2))1. This fragment is then integrated downstream of the CRISPR leading sequence to create spacer sequences with the assistance of enzymes like Cas1 and Cas2. The pre-crRNA and tracrRNA are transcribed using the leader sequence, followed by the formation of RNP complexes consisting of Cas9, crRNA, and tracrRNA. Thus, upon re-entry of the exogenous DNA into the bacterium, the RNP complexes can recognize the DNA _via_ the PAM sequence, it's worth noting that different CRISPR/Cas system means various PAM sequences. Subsequently, the RNP complexes can induce the double-stranded DNA break (DSB) using the HNH and RuvC nuclease domains in the Cas protein at the third base site adjacent to the upstream of the PAM sequence. Among that, the HNH nuclease structural domain is responsible for cleaving the DNA single-strand complementary to the crRNA strand, and the Ruvc nuclease domain cuts the non-complementary chains, leading to gene editing through non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) or homology-directed repair (HDR) mechanisms mediated gene insertion or deletion. Researchers can now achieve gene editing by creating several guide RNAs (sgRNAs) to target different sequences[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib1), [6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib6), [47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib47). Furthermore, using various CRISPR/Cas systems with PAM sequences allows researchers to achieve gene editing for more targets. The CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing method has become essential in the field of life sciences because of its efficiency, ease, and cost-effectiveness.

3. CRISPR/Cas9-based gene editing tools
---------------------------------------

Since the advent of the gene editing tools CRISPR/Cas9, significant efforts have been made to expand their application range and improve safety. Utilizing different forms of Cas-associated protein to alter the function is extensively studied[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib4), [6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib6), [48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib48), [49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib49), [50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib50). The CRISPR/Cas system can be categorized into two types based on the Cas-associated protein[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib4), [51](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib51). Class 1 typically involves the nucleic acid endonuclease function, which necessitates a multi-protein complex comprising types I, III, and IV. On the other hand, Class 2 employs a single Cas protein for DNA cleavage, which differs from Class 1. As a result, class 2 is more commonly used due to its simplicity. Cas9, a prevalent class 2 type II protein, consisting of two endonuclease domains: HNH and RuvC. Cas12 from class 2 type V only contains the Ruvc-like endonuclease domain, enabling it to cleave two target DNA strands. In contrast, class 2 type VI Cas13 exhibits RNA targeting capabilities instead of DNA targeting ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#fig3)A-D)[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib1), [9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib9), [10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib10), [47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib47).

In 2013, Qi et al.[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib52) showed that by introducing two silent mutations on the nuclease domains, RucC1 and HNH, they created a non-functional Cas9 lacking endonuclease activity, referred to as deadCas9 or dCas9. In this context, dCas9 maintains the ability to bind specific DNA sequences guided by sgRNA. Additionally, the target gene can be suppressed or activated by incorporating a transcriptional repressor or activator into dCas9, leading to the development of CRISPRi (CRISPR interference) and CRISPRa (CRISPR activation) ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#fig3)E and F)[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib9), [19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib19), [53](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib53). Various variations of transcription factors were explored for efficient gene expression regulation. The KRAB domain is the initial transcription repressor for CRISPRi systems, and dCas9-KRAB enhanced the inhibitory impact more than dCas9 alone[54](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib54). Later, additional transcription repressor domains were discovered[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib19). The CRISPRa system encompasses two classes: dCas9 coupled with a cis/trans-transcription activator domain. Initial experiments employing VP64 resulted in an upregulation of target gene transcription; however, the effect was limited[55](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib55). Moreover, the tripartite activator VP64-p65-Rta was mentioned. The dCas9-VP64 two-component system can be enhanced by utilizing the three-component systems SunTag (ScFv-(GCN4)-VP64) and SAM (MS2-p65-HSF1) for more potent activation of target genes[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib48), [56](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib56), [57](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib57), [58](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib58). CRISPRi/CRISPRa has transformed the role of CRISPR/Cas9 from gene editing to gene regulation. In addition to dCas9, Cas9 nickase (Cas9n), a variant of Cas9, typically with only one nucleic acid endonuclease structural domain, which induces single-stranded gaps rather than double-stranded breaks under the guidance of sgRNAs, can significantly reduce off-target effects[59](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib59).

CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing may lead to uncontrolled double-strand breaks (DSBs), particularly in certain contexts such as disorders connected to single gene mutations where DSBs are not desirable. Komor[60](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib60) originally reported the creation of the Cytidine Base Editor (CBE) in 2016, which enables the conversion from C-G to A-T by combining cytidine deaminase with dCas9. An Adenosine Base Editor (ABE), based on Cas9n, capable of changing A-T to G-C was developed just one-year later[61](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib61). The base editor has made significant advancements in accurate gene editing. However, other research has highlighted concerns over potential off-target effects and the possibility of gene mutations when using the base editor[62](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib62), [63](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib63), [64](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2211383525003132#bib64). enriched_points.expanded_content: Future CRISPR advancements include FDA-approved therapies for genetic diseases, improved precision editing techniques, and expanded applications in agriculture and regenerative medicine.
Ethical concerns include potential off-target effects, implications of germline editing, and equitable access to CRISPR technology, necessitating robust regulatory frameworks.
International collaborations can enhance CRISPR research, pooling resources and expertise to address global health challenges and ensure responsible gene editing practices.
